title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: reconciliation object records every name-matching decision. Inspect mapping see happened: detailed report:
code: result <- reconcile_tree(   x = avonet_subset,   tree = tree_jetz,   x_species = "Species1",   authority = NULL       # skip synonym lookup for speed ) #> ℹ Reconciling 919 data names vs 657 tree tips #> ℹ Matching 919 x 657 names through 2 stages... #> ℹ Stage 1/2: Exact matching... #> ℹ Stage 2/2: Normalised matching (0 matched so far)... #> ✔ Matched 657/919 data names to tree tips print(result) #>  #> ── Reconciliation: data vs tree ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   Source x: avonet_subset #>   Source y: phylo (657 tips) #>   Authority: none #>   Timestamp: 2026-05-01 18:34:54 #> ℹ Match coverage: [█████████████████████░░░░░░░░░] 71% (657/919) #>  #> ── Match summary ── #>  #> • Exact: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Normalized: 657 (71.5%) #> • Synonym: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Fuzzy: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Manual: 0 ( 0.0%) #> ! Unresolved (x only):262 (28.5%) #> ! Unresolved (y only):0 #> ! Flagged for review: 0 #> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table. mapping <- reconcile_mapping(result)  # Match type breakdown table(mapping$match_type) #>  #> normalized unresolved  #>        657        262  # Show normalised matches (formatting differences resolved automatically) norm <- mapping[mapping$match_type == "normalized",                 c("name_x", "name_y", "notes")] if (nrow(norm) > 0) head(norm, 5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   name_x                name_y                notes                              #>   <chr>                 <chr>                 <chr>                              #> 1 Acanthiza apicalis    Acanthiza_apicalis    'Acanthiza apicalis' normalised t… #> 2 Acanthiza chrysorrhoa Acanthiza_chrysorrhoa 'Acanthiza chrysorrhoa' normalise… #> 3 Acanthiza ewingii     Acanthiza_ewingii     'Acanthiza ewingii' normalised to… #> 4 Acanthiza inornata    Acanthiza_inornata    'Acanthiza inornata' normalised t… #> 5 Acanthiza iredalei    Acanthiza_iredalei    'Acanthiza iredalei' normalised t…  # Unresolved: in data but not in tree unresolved <- mapping[mapping$match_type == "unresolved" & mapping$in_x, ] cat(sprintf("\nSpecies in data but not in tree: %d\n", nrow(unresolved))) #>  #> Species in data but not in tree: 262 reconcile_summary(result, detail = "mismatches_only")

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: need random effects beyond phylogeny want Bayesian framework, use PGLMM. MCMCglmm package fits Bayesian phylogenetic mixed models: categorical responses (e.g., migration status multiple categories), see Mizuno et al. (2025, J. Evol. Biol. 38:1699–1715) multinomial PGLMM approach accompanying tutorial https://ayumi-495.github.io/multinomial-GLMM-tutorial/. See Hadfield (2010, J. Stat. Softw. 33:1–22) MCMCglmm details, Hadfield & Nakagawa (2010, J. Evol. Biol. 23:494–508) phylogenetic quantitative genetics, Mizuno et al. (2025, J. Evol. Biol. 38:1699–1715) phylogenetic multinomial mixed models. complete core workflow: load, reconcile, apply, analyse.
code: library(MCMCglmm)  # Species column as the phylogenetic grouping factor aligned$data$phylo <- aligned$data$Species1  # Inverse phylogenetic covariance matrix # Replace any zero-length branches (can arise after pruning) tree_mcmc <- aligned$tree tree_mcmc$edge.length[tree_mcmc$edge.length < .Machine$double.eps] <- 1e-6 inv_phylo <- inverseA(tree_mcmc, nodes = "ALL", scale = FALSE)  # PGLMM: continuous response prior <- list(R = list(V = 1, nu = 0.002),               G = list(G1 = list(V = 1, nu = 0.002)))  model_mcmc <- MCMCglmm(   log(Mass) ~ log(Wing.Length) + Trophic.Level,   random = ~phylo,   family = "gaussian",   ginverse = list(phylo = inv_phylo$Ainv),   data = aligned$data,   prior = prior,   nitt = 50000, burnin = 10000, thin = 20,   verbose = FALSE ) summary(model_mcmc) #>  #>  Iterations = 10001:49981 #>  Thinning interval  = 20 #>  Sample size  = 2000  #>  #>  DIC: -177.321  #>  #>  G-structure:  ~phylo #>  #>       post.mean l-95% CI u-95% CI eff.samp #> phylo   0.00168 0.001246 0.002134     1357 #>  #>  R-structure:  ~units #>  #>       post.mean l-95% CI u-95% CI eff.samp #> units    0.0316  0.02657  0.03692     1555 #>  #>  Location effects: log(Mass) ~ log(Wing.Length) + Trophic.Level  #>  #>                        post.mean l-95% CI u-95% CI eff.samp  pMCMC     #> (Intercept)             -6.23721 -6.77124 -5.74511     1916 <5e-04 *** #> log(Wing.Length)         2.15198  2.04803  2.25511     1829 <5e-04 *** #> Trophic.LevelHerbivore   0.02755 -0.05682  0.10939     2000  0.518     #> Trophic.LevelOmnivore    0.03632 -0.03227  0.10284     2000  0.271     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: need harmonise species names across two trait datasets matching tree, use reconcile_data(): reconciled, merge two datasets single data frame:
code: data(nesttrait_subset)   # Nest traits (Chia et al. 2023)  rec_data <- reconcile_data(   x = nesttrait_subset,   y = avonet_subset,   x_species = "Scientific_name",   y_species = "Species1",   authority = NULL,   quiet = TRUE ) print(rec_data) #>  #> ── Reconciliation: data vs data ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   Source x: nesttrait_subset #>   Source y: avonet_subset #>   Authority: none #>   Timestamp: 2026-05-01 18:35:09 #> ℹ Match coverage: [██████████████████████████████] 100% (916/916) #>  #> ── Match summary ── #>  #> • Exact: 916 (100.0%) #> • Normalized: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Synonym: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Fuzzy: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Manual: 0 ( 0.0%) #> ! Unresolved (x only):0 ( 0.0%) #> ! Unresolved (y only):3 #> ! Flagged for review: 0 #> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table. merged <- reconcile_merge(   rec_data,   data_x = nesttrait_subset,   data_y = avonet_subset,   species_col_x = "Scientific_name",   species_col_y = "Species1" ) #> ✔ Merged 916 species (inner join) cat(sprintf("Merged: %d rows, %d columns\n", nrow(merged), ncol(merged))) #> Merged: 916 rows, 31 columns

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: reconcile_merge() assumes one row per species data frame. species appears multiple rows (e.g. sex-specific measurements, repeated populations, individual-level records), merge produces pairwise combinations species — behaviour base merge(). reconcile_merge() warns detects duplicates surprised row expansion. two sensible ways handle multi-row data: Option . Aggregate first, merge second. downstream PCM expects one row per species (PGLS PGLMM workflows ), collapse species-level summary merging: Option B. Reconcile , join mapping back full data. want keep every row (e.g. individual-level PGLMM), build reconciliation species-level summary use mapping lookup table original, multi-row data:
code: # Example: averaging individual measurements to species means species_means <- aggregate(   cbind(Mass, Wing.Length) ~ Species1,   data = individual_measurements,   FUN  = mean ) merged <- reconcile_merge(rec_data, species_means, avonet_subset,                           species_col_x = "Species1",                           species_col_y = "Species1") # Reconcile on unique species species_level <- data.frame(   Species1 = unique(individual_measurements$Species1) ) rec <- reconcile_data(species_level, avonet_subset,                       x_species = "Species1", y_species = "Species1",                       authority = NULL, quiet = TRUE)  # Join the mapping back to the full, multi-row dataset mapping <- reconcile_mapping(rec) individual_measurements$species_resolved <- mapping$name_resolved[   match(individual_measurements$Species1, mapping$name_x) ]

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: common situation comparative biology merging small focal dataset much larger reference (e.g. field study 50 species AVONET’s ~10,000). reconcile_merge() accepts datasets size, argument matters: Use = "inner" analysis tolerate NAs reference columns, = "left" want retain every focal-study species (handle missingness model). = "full" rarely want — return entire reference dataset padded NAs every focal trait.
code: # Keep only species present in both: inner join inner <- reconcile_merge(rec_data, small_data, large_data,                          species_col_x = "species",                          species_col_y = "Species1",                          how = "inner")  # Keep all small_data rows; fill large_data columns with NA # for species missing from the reference: left join left <- reconcile_merge(rec_data, small_data, large_data,                         species_col_x = "species",                         species_col_y = "Species1",                         how = "left")

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: data tree use different taxonomies (e.g., BirdLife data BirdTree phylogeny), curated crosswalk can resolve names automated synonym resolution misses. crosswalk simply table mapping names one system another. prepR4pcm includes BirdLife-BirdTree crosswalk example: Convert overrides table pass reconcile_tree(): need crosswalk? data tree follow different naming authorities curated mapping exists. use cases, automatic cascade (exact → normalised → synonym) sufficient. can also build overrides manually — just data frame name_x, name_y, optionally user_note columns:
code: data(crosswalk_birdlife_birdtree) table(crosswalk_birdlife_birdtree$Match.type) #>  #>              1BL to 1BT          1BL to many BT                 Extinct  #>                    8960                     225                     143  #>          Many BL to 1BT Newly described species  #>                    1933                      24 overrides <- reconcile_crosswalk(   crosswalk_birdlife_birdtree,   from_col = "Species1",   to_col = "Species3",   match_type_col = "Match.type" ) #> ℹ 1933 many-to-one entries (lumps) included #> ℹ 225 one-to-many entries (splits) included #> ✔ Crosswalk: 3039 overrides (8079 identical pairs skipped)  # Re-reconcile with overrides result_xw <- reconcile_tree(   x = avonet_subset,   tree = tree_jetz,   x_species = "Species1",   authority = NULL,   overrides = overrides ) #> ℹ Reconciling 919 data names vs 657 tree tips #> ℹ Matching 919 x 657 names through 2 stages... #> ℹ Stage 1/2: Exact matching... #> ℹ Stage 2/2: Normalised matching (68 matched so far)... #> ! 2971 overrides could not be applied: 23 already_matched; 2767 name_x_not_in_data; 181 name_y_not_in_target. #> ℹ See `result$unused_overrides` for details. #> ✔ Matched 657/919 data names to tree tips  # Compare: how many more matches with the crosswalk? cat(sprintf("Without crosswalk: %d matched\n",             sum(result$mapping$in_x & result$mapping$in_y, na.rm = TRUE))) #> Without crosswalk: 657 matched cat(sprintf("With crosswalk:    %d matched\n",             sum(result_xw$mapping$in_x & result_xw$mapping$in_y, na.rm = TRUE))) #> With crosswalk:    657 matched my_overrides <- data.frame(   name_x    = c("Old name A", "Old name B"),   name_y    = c("Tree name A", "Tree name B"),   user_note = c("Reclassified in 2023", "Spelling correction") ) result <- reconcile_tree(my_data, my_tree, overrides = my_overrides)

text: sensitivity analyses across phylogenies, reconcile_to_trees() reconciles one dataset several trees one call:
code: data(tree_clements25)  # Clements 2025 tree  results <- reconcile_to_trees(   x = avonet_subset,   trees = list(     jetz      = tree_jetz,     clements  = tree_clements25   ),   x_species = "Species1",   authority = NULL ) #> ℹ Reconciling 919 data names against 2 trees #> ℹ   [jetz] 657 tips #> ℹ Matching 919 x 657 names through 2 stages... #> ℹ Stage 1/2: Exact matching... #> ℹ Stage 2/2: Normalised matching (0 matched so far)... #> ✔   [jetz] Matched 657/919 names #> ℹ   [clements] 854 tips #> ℹ Matching 919 x 854 names through 2 stages... #> ℹ Stage 1/2: Exact matching... #> ℹ Stage 2/2: Normalised matching (0 matched so far)... #> ✔   [clements] Matched 854/919 names  # Compare overlap across trees sapply(results, function(r) {   c(matched = sum(r$mapping$in_x & r$mapping$in_y, na.rm = TRUE),     unresolved_x = r$counts$n_unresolved_x) }) #>              jetz clements #> matched       657      854 #> unresolved_x  262       65

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: Enable fuzzy matching catch likely typos species names:
code: result <- reconcile_tree(   x = my_data,   tree = my_tree,   fuzzy = TRUE,              # enable fuzzy matching   fuzzy_threshold = 0.9,     # minimum similarity (0-1)   resolve = "flag"           # flag low-confidence matches for review )  # Check flagged matches flagged <- reconcile_mapping(result) flagged[flagged$match_type == "flagged", c("name_x", "name_y", "match_score")]

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: tree fewer species data, reconcile_apply() drops unresolved species. loses statistical power can bias sample. reconcile_augment() grafts missing species onto tree using genus-level placement: Use augmented tree downstream analyses. Pass augmented tree reconcile_apply() — existing reconciliation object still valid name-mapping key, new tree contains extra tips, drop_unresolved = FALSE retains grafted species: Important caveat. Genus-level placement assumes missing species diverged similarly congeners, may hold. Always report species augmented (aug$augmented) run sensitivity analyses comparing results without .
code:
aug <- reconcile_augment(   result,   tree_jetz,   where = "genus",                # sister to a random congener    branch_length = "congener_median",  # median terminal branch of congeners   seed = 42,                      # for reproducibility   quiet = TRUE ) #> Warning: Tree returned by "internal" is not strictly ultrametric. #> ℹ Most PCM methods (PGLS, BM, OU, etc.) assume ultrametric trees. #> → To force: `phytools::force.ultrametric(result$tree)` or #>   `ape::chronos(result$tree)`. #> • To suppress this check: pass `check_ultrametric = FALSE`.  cat(sprintf("Original tips: %d
Augmented tips: %d
",             ape::Ntip(aug$original), ape::Ntip(aug$tree))) #> Original tips: 657 #> Augmented tips: 793 cat(sprintf("Added: %d | Skipped (no congener): %d
",             nrow(aug$augmented), nrow(aug$skipped))) #> Added: 136 | Skipped (no congener): 126  # Which species were added, and where? if (nrow(aug$augmented) > 0) head(aug$augmented[, c("species", "placed_near", "branch_length")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   species                  placed_near             branch_length #>   <chr>                    <chr>                           <dbl> #> 1 Acanthiza cinerea        Acanthiza lineata                5.04 #> 2 Calamanthus cautus       Calamanthus fuliginosus          3.80 #> 3 Calamanthus montanellus  Calamanthus fuliginosus          3.80 #> 4 Calamanthus pyrrhopygius Calamanthus fuliginosus          3.80 #> 5 Gerygone citrina         Gerygone magnirostris            2.91 #> 6 Pyrrholaemus sagittatus  Pyrrholaemus brunneus           15.5 aligned_aug <- reconcile_apply(   result,   data = avonet_subset,   tree = aug$tree,        # augmented tree, not the original   species_col = "Species1",   drop_unresolved = FALSE  # keep augmented tips (they are now in the tree) )

title: From Raw Data to PCM: A Complete Bird Trait Workflow
text: Taxon-agnostic. workflow works group — mammals, fish, amphibians, plants — long data frame phylogenetic tree. Provenance. Every name-matching decision recorded reconciliation object. Use reconcile_summary() human-readable report reconcile_mapping() full table. Crosswalks optional. users need . automatic cascade handles formatting differences synonyms. Crosswalks help two well-known naming authorities disagree. Tree augmentation. tree incomplete, reconcile_augment() grafts missing species using congener placement — always run sensitivity analyses without augmented tips. Sensitivity. reconcile_to_trees() makes easy run analysis across multiple phylogenies. Merging. reconcile_merge() joins two reconciled datasets single analysis-ready data frame, using mapping join key. Reports. reconcile_report() generates self-contained HTML report suitable sharing archiving. Visualisation. reconcile_plot() produces bar pie chart match composition. reconcile_suggest() shows closest fuzzy candidates unresolved species. Comparison. reconcile_diff() compares two reconciliation runs side side — e.g., adding crosswalk.

title: Comparing tree backends — when do they agree?
text: Pick small species list. Retrieve set two three backends. Compare. three results independent. Now compare: print method shows three pairwise matrices. Reading : Tip-set Jaccard — fraction species trees place. 1.0 = species set. 0.0 = overlap. pairwise value < 1, ’re comparing apples apples. Robinson-Foulds distance — number internal edges differ two trees, restricted shared tip set. Lower = trees agree . Branch-length correlation — Pearson correlation sorted edge lengths pruning shared subtree. Useful rough check whether two chronograms agree rate scale.
code: species <- c("Salmo salar", "Esox lucius", "Oncorhynchus mykiss",              "Gadus morhua", "Thunnus thynnus")  # Three backends that all cover fish res_rotl     <- pr_get_tree(species, source = "rotl") res_fishtree <- pr_get_tree(species, source = "fishtree") res_rtrees   <- pr_get_tree(species, source = "rtrees", taxon = "fish") cmp <- pr_tree_compare(   rotl     = res_rotl,   fishtree = res_fishtree,   rtrees   = res_rtrees ) cmp

title: Comparing tree backends — when do they agree?
text: common question: “want VertLife / Upham et al. 2019 mammal posterior — backend ?” Short answer: source = "rtrees" . rtrees depends megatrees package, ships 100 randomly-sampled posterior trees major VertLife datasets (mammals: Upham et al. 2019; amphibians: Jetz & Pyron 2018; squamates: Tonini et al. 2016; sharks: Stein et al. 2018; birds: Jetz et al. 2012). call pr_get_tree(species, source = "rtrees", taxon = "mammal"), returned multiPhylo exactly 100-tree subset VertLife posterior, grafted species set. need full 10,000-tree posterior (rather 100-tree subset), currently download source archive manually vertlife.org (archive 0.5–2 GB). future round may add source = "vertlife" backend automates caching step; now, 100-tree subset via rtrees get box, sufficient great majority phylogenetic comparative analyses.

title: Comparing tree backends — when do they agree?
text: backends disagree species valid. Inspect unique-lists: Common causes: - Taxonomy difference. rotl uses Open Tree taxonomy; fishtree uses ; clootl uses Clements. species may different canonical names. - Coverage gap. species one reference tree another (often recently described species). - Synonymy. One backend matches via synonymy, another doesn’t. Mitigation: re-run tnrs = "always" harmonise names via Open Tree’s TNRS first. See ?pr_get_tree.
code: cmp$unique_to$rotl     # species rotl placed but the others didn't cmp$unique_to$fishtree # species fishtree placed but the others didn't cmp$shared_tips        # species placed by all backends

Usually one time-calibrated isn’t: rotl returns synthesis tree calibration. rtrees grafts onto reference tree; calibration whatever reference uses. fishtree clootl datelife return time-calibrated trees. downstream model expects calibrated branches (BM / OU / lambda models ), use calibrated backend. calibrated option taxon datelife, see ?pr_date_tree dating existing topology.

title: Comparing tree backends — when do they agree?

title: Comparing tree backends — when do they agree?
text: Bipartition-matched branch-length correlation. current implementation correlates sorted edge lengths, coarse defensible “agree rate scale” check. proper bipartition-matched correlation future-round refinement. Visualisation. Pair phytools::cophyloplot() phangorn::densiTree() visual comparison two many trees. Three---tree consensus. “’s median tree across three backends?”, use phangorn::consensus(c(res_rotl$tree, res_fishtree$tree, res_rtrees$tree[[1]])).

title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: package bundles three small source tables one small tree. source tables sampled real database structures, using columns needed example. mirror three common inputs mammal comparative work: Amniote life-history database (Myhrvold et al. 2015), PanTHERIA (Jones et al. 2009), TetrapodTraits (Moura et al. 2024).
code:
data(mammal_amniote_example) data(mammal_pantheria_example) data(mammal_tetrapodtraits_example) data(mammal_tree_example)  cat(sprintf("Amniote-like source:        %d rows
", nrow(mammal_amniote_example))) #> Amniote-like source:        4953 rows cat(sprintf("PanTHERIA-like source:      %d rows
", nrow(mammal_pantheria_example))) #> PanTHERIA-like source:      5416 rows cat(sprintf("TetrapodTraits-like source: %d rows
", nrow(mammal_tetrapodtraits_example))) #> TetrapodTraits-like source: 5911 rows cat(sprintf("Tree:                       %d tips
", ape::Ntip(mammal_tree_example))) #> Tree:                       5987 tips

title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: three sources contain related information, use column names. typical assembling trait data independent databases.
code: source_columns <- tibble::tibble(   source = c("Amniote", "PanTHERIA", "TetrapodTraits"),   n_rows = c(     nrow(mammal_amniote_example),     nrow(mammal_pantheria_example),     nrow(mammal_tetrapodtraits_example)   ),   n_columns = c(     ncol(mammal_amniote_example),     ncol(mammal_pantheria_example),     ncol(mammal_tetrapodtraits_example)   ),   species_column = c("name", "MSW05_Binomial", "Scientific.Name") )  knitr::kable(source_columns)

title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: first convert source long-format structure. result keeps one row per source record, plus source column provenance value retained. can now check much information source contributes.
code: amniote_std <- prep_source(   mammal_amniote_example,   source_name     = "AMNIOTE",   species_col     = "name",   female_mass_col = "female_body_mass_g",   adult_mass_col  = "adult_body_mass_g",   litter_size_col = "litter_or_clutch_size_n",   litter_y_col    = "litters_or_clutches_per_y" )  pantheria_std <- prep_source(   mammal_pantheria_example,   source_name     = "PANTHERIA",   species_col     = "MSW05_Binomial",   adult_mass_col  = "5-1_AdultBodyMass_g",   litter_size_col = "15-1_LitterSize",   litter_y_col    = "16-1_LittersPerYear" )  tetrapodtraits_std <- prep_source(   mammal_tetrapodtraits_example,   source_name     = "TETRAPODTRAITS",   species_col     = "Scientific.Name",   adult_mass_col  = "BodyMass_g",   litter_size_col = "LitterSize" )  db_long_raw <- bind_rows(   amniote_std,   pantheria_std,   tetrapodtraits_std )  knitr::kable(slice_head(db_long_raw, n = 10)) source_coverage <- db_long_raw |>   group_by(source) |>   summarise(     n_records           = n(),     n_species           = n_distinct(species),     adult_mass_records  = sum(!is.na(adult_mass_g)),     female_mass_records = sum(!is.na(female_mass_g)),     litter_size_records = sum(!is.na(litter_size_n)),     litter_y_records    = sum(!is.na(litters_per_year_n)),     .groups = "drop"   )  knitr::kable(source_coverage)

what: Step 3: Reconcile species names with the tree
title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: Name reconciliation done unique source names, every row trait database. creates one matching decision per raw species name. now reconcile source names tree. External synonym lookup turned vignette remains fast reproducible. mapping table records source names matched tree need review. Names remain unresolved flagged can inspected separately. also ask suggested matches. suggestions applied automatically; candidates manual review.
code:
species_lookup <- db_long_raw |>   distinct(species) |>   rename(species_raw = species)  knitr::kable(slice_head(species_lookup, n = 10)) rec0 <- reconcile_tree(   x         = species_lookup,   tree      = mammal_tree_example,   x_species = "species_raw",   authority = NULL,   fuzzy     = FALSE,   quiet     = TRUE )  reconcile_summary(rec0, detail = "brief") #>  #> === Reconciliation Report === #> Type: data_tree #> Timestamp: 2026-05-01 18:35:21 #> Package: prepR4pcm 0.4.0.9000 #> Authority: NONE (version: latest) #> Rank: species #>  #> --- Match Summary --- #>   Exact:       4677 / 11304 #>   Normalized:  1234 / 11304 #>   Synonym:     0 / 11304 #>   Fuzzy:       0 / 11304 #>   Manual:      0 / 11304 #>   Unresolved:  5393 (x only) + 76 (y only) #> mapping0 <- reconcile_mapping(rec0)  mapping_preview <- mapping0 |>   select(any_of(c("name_x", "name_y", "match_type", "in_x", "in_y"))) |>   arrange(match_type, name_x) |>   slice_head(n = 15)  knitr::kable(mapping_preview) review_names <- mapping0 |>   filter(in_x, match_type %in% c("unresolved", "flagged")) |>   arrange(match_type, name_x)  if (nrow(review_names) == 0) {   cat("No unresolved or flagged names in this example.
") } else {   cat(sprintf(     "Showing 10 of %d unresolved or flagged names.

",     nrow(review_names)   ))   knitr::kable(slice_head(review_names, n = 10) |>                  select(any_of(c("name_x", "name_y", "match_type",                                  "in_x", "in_y")))) } #> Showing 10 of 5393 unresolved or flagged names. suggestions0 <- reconcile_suggest(rec0, n = 3, threshold = 0.9) #> ✔ Found suggestions for 4683 of 5393 unresolved species.  suggestions_to_review <- suggestions0 |>   transmute(     name_x = unresolved,     name_y = suggestion,     score  = score   ) |>   filter(score >= 0.9, score < 1) |>   arrange(desc(score))  if (nrow(suggestions_to_review) == 0) {   cat("No high-confidence, non-perfect suggestions were found.
") } else {   cat(     "Showing up to 10 high-confidence suggested matches with score below 1.

",     sep = ""   )   knitr::kable(slice_head(suggestions_to_review, n = 10), digits = 3) } #> Showing up to 10 high-confidence suggested matches with score below 1.

title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: Automated reconciliation useful, names still need human review. suggestion table helps identify likely matches. Manual corrections stored small editable table. important rule simple: name_x must name trait database, name_y must exact tip label tree. apply manual corrections reconciliation table. manual_overrides empty, automated mapping kept unchanged. corrected mapping can now joined back full source-level trait table.
code: manual_overrides <- suggestions_to_review |>   slice_head(n = 2) |>   mutate(user_note = "Accepted from high-confidence reconciliation suggestion") |>   select(name_x, name_y, user_note)  if (nrow(manual_overrides) == 0) {   cat("No manual corrections were added in this example.\n") } else {   knitr::kable(manual_overrides, digits = 3) } mapping_final <- mapping0 |>   left_join(     manual_overrides |>       rename(manual_name_y = name_y, manual_note = user_note),     by = "name_x"   ) |>   mutate(     species_tree    = coalesce(manual_name_y, name_y),     matched_to_tree = species_tree %in% mammal_tree_example$tip.label,     match_type      = if_else(!is.na(manual_name_y), "manual", match_type),     notes           = manual_note   ) |>   select(-manual_name_y, -manual_note) final_reconciliation_summary <- mapping_final |>   filter(in_x) |>   count(match_type, name = "n_names") |>   arrange(desc(n_names), match_type)  knitr::kable(final_reconciliation_summary) name_map <- mapping_final |>   filter(in_x) |>   transmute(     species_raw     = name_x,     species_tree    = species_tree,     matched_to_tree = matched_to_tree,     match_type      = match_type,     notes           = notes   )  db_full <- db_long_raw |>   rename(species_raw = species) |>   left_join(name_map, by = "species_raw") |>   relocate(source, row_in_source, species_raw, species_tree,            matched_to_tree, match_type)  db_tree_matched <- db_full |>   filter(matched_to_tree, !is.na(species_tree))  knitr::kable(   db_tree_matched |>     select(source, species_raw, species_tree, match_type,            adult_mass_g, female_mass_g, litter_size_n,            litters_per_year_n) |>     slice_head(n = 10),   digits = 3 )

what: Step 5: Collapse to one row per species
title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: source-level database can now summarised one record per matched species. use median trait value across available source records keep simple provenance columns.
code: db_species_summary <- db_tree_matched |>   group_by(species_tree) |>   summarise(     n_sources_total       = n_distinct(source),     sources               = safe_sources(source),     adult_mass_g          = safe_median(adult_mass_g),     female_mass_g         = safe_median(female_mass_g),     litter_size_n         = safe_median(litter_size_n),     litters_per_year_n    = safe_median(litters_per_year_n),     adult_mass_n_records  = sum(!is.na(adult_mass_g)),     female_mass_n_records = sum(!is.na(female_mass_g)),     litter_size_n_records = sum(!is.na(litter_size_n)),     litter_y_n_records    = sum(!is.na(litters_per_year_n)),     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   mutate(annual_offspring_n = litter_size_n * litters_per_year_n) trait_coverage <- db_species_summary |>   summarise(     n_species               = n(),     adult_mass_species      = sum(!is.na(adult_mass_g)),     female_mass_species     = sum(!is.na(female_mass_g)),     litter_size_species     = sum(!is.na(litter_size_n)),     litters_per_year_species= sum(!is.na(litters_per_year_n)),     annual_offspring_species= sum(!is.na(annual_offspring_n))   )  knitr::kable(trait_coverage)

what: Step 6: Align the database and the tree
title: Assembling mammal trait databases for phylogenetic comparative models
text: phylogenetic comparative models, data tree must refer species. prune tree order data rows match tree tips.
code: matched_tips <- intersect(   mammal_tree_example$tip.label,   db_species_summary$species_tree )  tree_pcm <- keep.tip(mammal_tree_example, matched_tips)  pcm_data <- db_species_summary |>   filter(species_tree %in% tree_pcm$tip.label) |>   mutate(species = species_tree) |>   arrange(match(species, tree_pcm$tip.label)) |>   relocate(species)  stopifnot(identical(pcm_data$species, tree_pcm$tip.label)) alignment_check <- tibble::tibble(   object          = c("pcm_data", "tree_pcm"),   species_or_tips = c(nrow(pcm_data), ape::Ntip(tree_pcm)),   aligned         = c(     identical(pcm_data$species, tree_pcm$tip.label),     identical(pcm_data$species, tree_pcm$tip.label)   ) )  knitr::kable(alignment_check)

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: Mismatches data tree arise three kinds difference: Formatting differences — species, written differently. example, animal may appear Homo_sapiens tree Homo sapiens data; trailing whitespace attached authority strings (Homo sapiens Linnaeus, 1758) cause similar mismatches. Synonymy — situations multiple scientific names refer taxonomic group (often species genus); example, recent taxonomic revision moved species different genus, older newer names circulate literature. See Synonym (taxonomy) Wikipedia fuller introduction. Missing names — species data tree, tree data, naming-rule link . Fixing hand tedious, error-prone, poorly documented. prepR4pcm solves structured matching cascade algorithms: exact match → normalised match → synonym resolution. Every decision recorded software reconciliation result, can inspect via reconcile_mapping() reconcile_summary().

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
code: # Install pak if you don't have it # install.packages("pak")  # Install prepR4pcm from GitHub pak::pak("itchyshin/prepR4pcm") library(prepR4pcm)

what: Example 1: Reconcile a dataset against a tree
title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: Suppose trait data phylogenetic tree slightly different naming conventions.  (ape::plot.phylo() displays underscores spaces default — underlying tree$tip.label strings still contain underscores, tree$tip.label shows .) Notice mismatches: Pan_troglodytes data underscore; tree uses underscores throughout, data column mixes spaces underscores. Cebus capucinus data tree. Papio anubis tree data.
code: # Simulated trait data for 6 primate species trait_data <- data.frame(   species = c(     "Homo sapiens",     "Pan_troglodytes",       # underscore instead of space     "Gorilla gorilla",     "Pongo pygmaeus",     "Macaca mulatta",     "Cebus capucinus"   ),   body_mass = c(70, 50, 160, 80, 8, 3),   brain_mass = c(1.35, 0.39, 0.50, 0.37, 0.11, 0.07) )  # Simulated phylogenetic tree (built manually for this example) tree <- ape::read.tree(text = paste0(   "((((Homo_sapiens:5,Pan_troglodytes:5):3,",   "Gorilla_gorilla:8):4,Pongo_pygmaeus:12):6,",   "(Macaca_mulatta:10,Papio_anubis:10):8);" ))  tree$tip.label   # the tip labels (species names) on the tree #> [1] "Homo_sapiens"    "Pan_troglodytes" "Gorilla_gorilla" "Pongo_pygmaeus"  #> [5] "Macaca_mulatta"  "Papio_anubis" plot(tree)       # quick visual; underscores in tip labels render as spaces result <- reconcile_tree(   x = trait_data,   tree = tree,   x_species = "species",   authority = NULL,        # skip synonym lookup for this example   quiet = FALSE ) #> ℹ Reconciling 6 data names vs 6 tree tips #> ✔ Matched 5/6 data names to tree tips

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: “Reconciliation: data vs tree” header top output tells call produced result; “Match summary” block underneath gives count match category (exact, normalised, synonym, fuzzy, manual, unresolved). Use reconcile_mapping() see full per-name table: columns mean: name_x — species name appeared data (argument x reconcile_tree()). name_y — matching tip label tree (argument tree reconcile_tree()), NA match found. name_resolved — canonical name used synonym resolution applied (recognised form per chosen taxonomic authority). NA matches didn’t go synonym stage. match_type — stage cascade matched name (see Understanding match types ). match_score — confidence [0, 1] (1 exact / normalised / synonym / manual; < 1 fuzzy / flagged). in_x, in_y — logical: name data, tree, ? notes — human-readable note (e.g. “normalised: lowercased”, “via synonym lookup COL”, “fuzzy match score 0.92”). detailed report:
code: print(result) #>  #> ── Reconciliation: data vs tree ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   Source x: trait_data #>   Source y: phylo (6 tips) #>   Authority: none #>   Timestamp: 2026-05-01 18:35:30 #> ℹ Match coverage: [█████████████████████████░░░░░] 83% (5/6) #>  #> ── Match summary ── #>  #> • Exact: 1 (16.7%) #> • Normalized: 4 (66.7%) #> • Synonym: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Fuzzy: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Manual: 0 ( 0.0%) #> ! Unresolved (x only):1 (16.7%) #> ! Unresolved (y only):1 #> ! Flagged for review: 0 #> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table. reconcile_mapping(result) #> # A tibble: 7 × 9 #>   name_x    name_y name_resolved match_type match_score match_source in_x  in_y  #>   <chr>     <chr>  <chr>         <chr>            <dbl> <chr>        <lgl> <lgl> #> 1 Pan_trog… Pan_t… NA            exact                1 exact_string TRUE  TRUE  #> 2 Homo sap… Homo_… NA            normalized           1 normalisati… TRUE  TRUE  #> 3 Gorilla … Goril… NA            normalized           1 normalisati… TRUE  TRUE  #> 4 Pongo py… Pongo… NA            normalized           1 normalisati… TRUE  TRUE  #> 5 Macaca m… Macac… NA            normalized           1 normalisati… TRUE  TRUE  #> 6 Cebus ca… NA     NA            unresolved          NA NA           TRUE  FALSE #> 7 NA        Papio… NA            unresolved          NA NA           FALSE TRUE  #> # ℹ 1 more variable: notes <chr> reconcile_summary(result)

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: Suppose know Cebus capucinus analysis. can document decision: reconcile_override() updates existing result (reconciliation built earlier) place — need re-run reconcile_tree(). three actions can pass action = ... : "accept" — confirm specific name_x → name_y mapping. "reject" — mark name deliberately excluded. "replace" — redirect name_x different name_y cascade produced.

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: prepR4pcm can also reconcile species names two datasets, just dataset tree. matching cascade applies. useful merging trait data different sources, species names often disagree across datasets. toy example: Pan_troglodytes (underscore) df1 matched Pan troglodytes (space) df2 via normalisation. Gorilla gorilla df1 Pongo pygmaeus df2 — end unresolved rows (in_x = TRUE, in_y = FALSE vice versa).
code: # df1: body mass for three primates (df1 uses an underscore for chimp) df1 <- data.frame(   species = c("Homo sapiens", "Pan_troglodytes", "Gorilla gorilla"),   mass = c(70, 50, 160) )  # df2: lifespan for three primates (df2 uses a space for chimp; orang # is here but not gorilla) df2 <- data.frame(   species = c("Homo sapiens", "Pan troglodytes", "Pongo pygmaeus"),   lifespan = c(79, 40, 45) )  # Reconcile the species columns of df1 and df2 against each other. # `authority = NULL` skips the synonym-lookup stage (no taxonomic # database needed for this small example). `quiet = TRUE` suppresses # progress messages. result2 <- reconcile_data(   x = df1,   y = df2,   authority = NULL,   quiet = TRUE ) #> ℹ Auto-detected species column: species #> ℹ Auto-detected species column: species  # The output shows how many names matched, and via which stage. print(result2) #>  #> ── Reconciliation: data vs data ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   Source x: df1 #>   Source y: df2 #>   Authority: none #>   Timestamp: 2026-05-01 18:35:31 #> ℹ Match coverage: [████████████████████░░░░░░░░░░] 67% (2/3) #>  #> ── Match summary ── #>  #> • Exact: 1 (33.3%) #> • Normalized: 1 (33.3%) #> • Synonym: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Fuzzy: 0 ( 0.0%) #> • Manual: 0 ( 0.0%) #> ! Unresolved (x only):1 (33.3%) #> ! Unresolved (y only):1 #> ! Flagged for review: 0 #> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table.

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: taxonomic authority curated database species names records, name, currently-recognised one synonyms (alternative names referring taxon). prepR4pcm can use authority recognise two syntactically different names refer species — “synonym” stage matching cascade. supported authorities (served via taxadb): col — Catalogue Life (catalogueoflife.org). Broad coverage; general default cross-taxon work. itis — Integrated Taxonomic Information System (itis.gov). North-American emphasis, strong vertebrates vascular plants. gbif — Global Biodiversity Information Facility taxonomic backbone (gbif.org/dataset). Pragmatic synthesis wide coverage. ncbi — NCBI Taxonomy (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/taxonomy). Tracks names appear GenBank — useful molecular-data workflows. ott — Open Tree Taxonomy (opentreeoflife.org//taxonomy-version). taxonomy used Open Tree Life, useful paired rotl retrieval backend. itis_test — small bundled subset ITIS used package’s examples tests; general-purpose authority. mirror providers documented ?taxadb::td_create. database downloaded first use cached locally, ensuring reproducibility (results don’t depend external API available day run analysis). set authority? Use authority = NULL (skip synonym lookup) : want quick offline check — database download required. Species names data tree unlikely differ much (formatting differences caught normalisation stage anyway). Set authority = "col" (another provider) names differ genuine taxonomic revisions — species moved different genus, splits, lumps. first run downloads local database (~100 MB); subsequent runs fast database cached.
code: # Requires taxadb and a local database download (automatic on first use) result3 <- reconcile_tree(   x = trait_data,   tree = tree,   x_species = "species",   authority = "col"        # Catalogue of Life )

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: Researchers often maintain curated list known corrections. can pass data frame, path file CSV format: chunks use my_data my_tree hypothetical objects (substitute data frame phylo object). marked eval = FALSE vignette renders without requiring objects exist. Overrides applied matching stage, always take priority.
code: # A data frame of known corrections corrections <- data.frame(   name_x = c("Corvus sp.", "Turdus merulaa"),   name_y = c("Corvus corax", "Turdus merula"),   user_note = c("Only one Corvus in our tree", "Typo in source data") )  result4 <- reconcile_tree(   x = my_data,   tree = my_tree,   overrides = corrections )  # Or from a CSV file: result5 <- reconcile_tree(   x = my_data,   tree = my_tree,   overrides = "lab_corrections.csv" )

what: Example 5: Multiple datasets against one tree
title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: reconcile_multi() reconciles several datasets , pooling unique species names running cascade:
code: # Suppose you have several data frames to reconcile against one tree. # `my_ecology_data`, `my_morpho_data`, and `my_tree` are **hypothetical** # user-supplied objects; substitute your own. datasets <- list(   traits  = trait_data,        # defined above   ecology = my_ecology_data,   # your own data frame   morpho  = my_morpho_data     # your own data frame )  result6 <- reconcile_multi(datasets, my_tree) print(result6)

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: Conservative: Names never silently changed. Ambiguous cases flagged, auto-resolved. Transparent: Every decision recorded match type, score, source, human-readable note. Reproducible: Database versions pinned. parameters used build result stored result object , collaborator can re-run reconciliation later. Practical: Works data types comparative biologists already use — data.frame trait values (one row per species) phylogenetic tree ape::phylo object.

title: Getting Started with prepR4pcm
text: chunk uses hypothetical files (species_traits.csv, species_tree.nwk) — substitute paths. chunk marked eval = FALSE doesn’t try read files don’t exist vignette rendered.
code: library(prepR4pcm)  # 1. Load your data and tree (hypothetical paths -- substitute your own) my_data <- read.csv("species_traits.csv") my_tree <- ape::read.tree("species_tree.nwk")  # 2. Reconcile result <- reconcile_tree(my_data, my_tree, authority = "col")  # 3. Review print(result) reconcile_summary(result, detail = "mismatches_only")  # 4. Fix manually if needed result <- reconcile_override(result, "Corvus sp.", "Corvus corax",                              note = "Only one Corvus in tree")  # 5. Apply aligned <- reconcile_apply(result, data = my_data, tree = my_tree,                             drop_unresolved = TRUE)  # 6. Analyse # aligned$data and aligned$tree are ready for caper, phytools, MCMCglmm, etc.

what: A worked example: thermal-tolerance plasticity across animal classes
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: ’ll mirror phylogeny construction Pottier et al. (2022) “Developmental plasticity thermal tolerance” (Ecology Letters 25(10):2245-2268, doi:10.1111/ele.14083). dataset spans 147 ectotherm species across 14 animal classes — fish, insects, reptiles, amphibians, crustaceans, bivalves, echinoderms, cephalopods, etc. exactly cross-taxon case rotl practical option (taxon-specific mega-tree covers 14 classes). brevity, ’ll use 13 representative species — one two major class Pottier dataset:

title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
code: library(prepR4pcm) # Suggested for the analysis itself: # install.packages("rotl")     # OToL access # install.packages("metafor")  # the meta-analysis engine

what: Step 1-3: topology -> bifurcating -> Grafen branches, in one call
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: Pottier et al. methods description matches almost exactly; wrote pipeline rotl::tnrs_match_names() → rotl::tol_induced_subtree() → ape::multi2di(random = TRUE) → ape::compute.brlen(method = "Grafen", power = 1) → ape::vcv(cor = TRUE). two new arguments pr_get_tree() collapse four lines (steps 2-4) one. two new arguments — resolve_polytomies branch_lengths — collapse otherwise three explicit pipeline steps:
code: species <- c(   # Actinopterygii (ray-finned fish)   "Salmo trutta",              "Acipenser fulvescens",   # Reptilia   "Anolis sagrei",   # Amphibia   "Xenopus laevis",            "Rhinella marina",   # Insecta   "Ischnura elegans",          "Anopheles albimanus",   # Malacostraca (crustaceans)   "Litopenaeus vannamei",   # Branchiopoda   "Daphnia magna",   # Bivalvia (mussels)   "Lampsilis abrupta",   # Echinoidea (sea urchins)   "Mesocentrotus franciscanus",   # Cephalopoda   "Octopus maya",   # Chondrichthyes (sharks/rays)   "Heterodontus portusjacksoni",   # Hyperoartia (lampreys)   "Petromyzon marinus" )  res <- pr_get_tree(   species,   source             = "rotl",   resolve_polytomies = TRUE,        # multi2di(random = TRUE)   branch_lengths     = "grafen"     # compute.brlen(method = "Grafen") ) res$tree           # ultrametric, bifurcating, Grafen branches res$matched        # species OToL successfully resolved res$unmatched      # species OToL couldn't place # What `pr_get_tree(... resolve_polytomies = TRUE, #                       branch_lengths   = "grafen")` # is equivalent to (when source = "rotl"): res <- pr_get_tree(species, source = "rotl")  # rotl topology tr  <- res$tree tr  <- ape::multi2di(tr, random = TRUE)        # bifurcate tr  <- ape::compute.brlen(tr, method = "Grafen") res$tree <- tr

title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: pr_phylo_cor() thin wrapper around ape::vcv(tree, corr = TRUE). function exists separately meta-analysis pattern self-documenting matrix can carry tree provenance.
code: phy_cor <- pr_phylo_cor(res) dim(phy_cor)            # 9 x 9 (one row/col per species) isSymmetric(phy_cor)    # TRUE all(diag(phy_cor) == 1) # TRUE (correlation matrix; ultrametric tree)

what: Step 5: fit the meta-analysis with metafor
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: Pottier-style analysis, effect size per study log response ratio (lnRR) CTmax contrasting developmental temperatures. Sampling variance comes effect via group sample sizes / SDs. Suppose ’ve already extracted per-study, per-species (realistic shape ectotherm-thermal-tolerance dataset): R = list(species = phy_cor) argument tells metafor species random effect correlation structure given phylogeny. ~1 | study partitions within-study -species variance — standard meta-analysis multi-study effect-size data. Pottier et al. used slightly complex metafor model including population, family, shared-trial random effects (see Statistical_analyses.Rmd). phylogenetic component R = list(species = phy_cor) either way.
code: library(metafor)  # Toy effect-size data --- one row per study; one or more rows per # species. Replace with your real extraction. set.seed(42) toy_df <- data.frame(   species = sample(species, 30, replace = TRUE),   study   = sprintf("study_%02d", seq_len(30)),   yi      = rnorm(30, mean = 0.20, sd = 0.15),   # lnRR   vi      = runif(30, min = 0.01, max = 0.08)    # sampling variance )  # Fit: random study effect (within-study) + random species effect # (between-species, with phylogenetic correlation structure). fit <- rma.mv(   yi ~ 1,   vi,   random  = list(~1 | study, ~1 | species),   R       = list(species = phy_cor),   data    = toy_df,   method  = "REML" ) summary(fit)

what: Manual additions when OToL doesn’t have a species
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: common situation: OToL doesn’t one species. Pottier et al. ran Potamilus alatus (freshwater mussel) — OToL’s induced subtree errored , pulled tnrs match, generated tree without , grafted back manually ape::bind.tip(): prepR4pcm, equivalent “graft species back ” reconcile_augment(). pr_get_tree() gives OToL-resolved tree, call reconcile_augment() residually unresolved species; genus-level (MRCA-level) placement automatically records placement $augmented methods section: one-species-manually-added pattern shows Cinar et al. (2022, Methods Ecol. Evol. 13:383), Villosa delumbis added based ITIS — describe “Polytomies resolved random, one species, Villosa delumbis, manually added tree based information Integrated Taxonomic Information System.”
code: # Pottier et al.'s approach (paraphrased from their Rmd) taxa.tree <- filter(taxa, ott_id != "732215") # remove Potamilus alatus phylo_tree <- tol_induced_subtree(ott_ids = taxa.tree$ott_id) binary.tree <- multi2di(phylo_tree, random = TRUE) binary.tree <- bind.tip(binary.tree, tip.label = "Potamilus_alatus",                         where = 95)            # grafted into Bivalvia # Step 0 in your workflow: reconcile data names against the tree. # Any species OToL didn't place lands in `unresolved`; you can # graft them onto the tree as sister to a congener (genus-level # placement; cite the taxonomic source for the placement, just as # Cinar et al. cite ITIS). rec <- reconcile_data(my_effect_size_df, x_species = "species",                        authority = "itis") aug <- reconcile_augment(rec, res$tree)   # graft unresolved species phy_cor <- pr_phylo_cor(aug$tree)

what: Why Grafen (and not datelife / a time-calibrated tree)?
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: principle replace Grafen branches biologically real ones datelife: phylogenetic meta-analyses, recommend Grafen, datelife, three reasons: Coverage. Datelife synthesises per-clade published chronograms. cross-taxon dataset like Pottier’s (lampreys, bivalves, insects, fish, mammals etc., spanning 14 animal classes), datelife usually can’t return chronogram covering — database sparse outside well-studied single-clade groups (mammals, birds, fish). Question alignment. Phylogenetic meta-analysis models species random effect phylogenetic correlation structure. model asks “related species correlated effect sizes?”, “fast trait evolve per unit time?”. Pagel’s λ species random effect (metafor estimates implicitly supply correlation matrix) absorbs branch-length scale, choice Grafen-units time-units matters less , say, Brownian-motion rate estimate. Reproducibility. Grafen’s method deterministic given topology; datelife results depend chronograms database day run . Use datelife instead : taxa fall within single, well-studied clade (mammals, birds, insects-Lepidoptera, fish — see ?pr_date_tree). science question requires real divergence times (trait- evolution rate, Brownian-motion variance per million years, divergence-time questions). ’re fitting Brownian-motion--tree model directly (e.g. caper::pgls phylolm::phylolm) branch-length scale enters variance directly. realistic cross-taxon meta-analysis case (one Pottier et al. 2022 many others run), Grafen via pr_get_tree(..., branch_lengths = "grafen") practical default.
code: # Alternative: get rotl topology, then date it with datelife res    <- pr_get_tree(species, source = "rotl",                       resolve_polytomies = TRUE)   # NO branch_lengths dated  <- pr_date_tree(res$tree)                   # datelife dates it phy_cor <- pr_phylo_cor(dated)

what: What this vignette deliberately doesn’t cover
title: Phylogenetic meta-analysis with rotl + prepR4pcm
text: Journal Statistical Software 36:1. Branch-length sensitivity. Cinar et al. simulation shows Grafen-vs-time-calibrated lengths matter. topology time-calibrated (e.g. source = "fishtree" source = "datelife"), don’t apply Grafen top — pass branch_lengths = NULL (default) use chronogram directly. Multi-tree posterior. backend returns multiPhylo, pr_phylo_cor() returns list correlation matrices. Feed metafor separately pool Rubin’s rules — see posterior-tree pipeline vignette.

text: Start whatever names appear dataset. never quite match tree’s tip labels.
code: # Your data and your tree — typically loaded from disk my_data <- data.frame(   species   = c("Salmo salar", "Esox lucius", "Esox_lucius",                 "Oncorhynchus mykiss"),   body_size = c(98, 130, NA, 50)   # NA is OK; pigauto will impute )  # Reconcile names against a published taxonomic authority. Catches # typos, formatting drift, and synonymy. rec <- reconcile_data(my_data,                       x_species = "species",                       authority = "col")  # Catalogue of Life rec   # one-line summary of matched / unmatched / counts

what: Step 2 — Get a posterior of trees
title: From species names to a phylogenetic posterior — prepR4pcm + pigauto
text: headline change Round 5: every backend supports n_tree > 1, every backend can return multiple trees . ask fishtree 50 stochastic resolutions (Chang, Rabosky, Alfaro 2019, Syst. Biol.). universal--slower option, use source = "datelife" — chronogram-database backend returns one calibrated tree per source (Hedges, Bininda-Emonds, Upham, etc.). already topology want date (rather fetch new one), use pr_date_tree():
code: trees <- pr_get_tree(rec,                      source = "fishtree",                      n_tree = 50)  class(trees$tree)             # "multiPhylo" length(trees$tree)            # 50 length(trees$matched)         # how many species placed head(trees$unmatched)         # species absent from fishtree  # Per-tree provenance: one entry per tree length(trees$backend_meta$tree_provenance)  # 50 trees$backend_meta$tree_provenance[[1]] trees <- pr_get_tree(rec,                      source = "datelife",                      n_tree = 50) # trees$tree is multiPhylo; per-source citations are in # trees$backend_meta$tree_provenance[[i]]$citation my_topology   <- ape::read.tree("my_topology.nex") dated_trees   <- pr_date_tree(my_topology, n_dated = 50) class(dated_trees$tree)        # multiPhylo of 50 dated copies

what: Step 3 — Format citations (do this before you submit)
title: From species names to a phylogenetic posterior — prepR4pcm + pigauto
text: Reviewers ask. Save round-trip.
code: # Plain text for an email or a methods paragraph pr_cite_tree(trees)  # Markdown for a GitHub issue or PR description cat(pr_cite_tree(trees, format = "markdown"))  # BibTeX for a manuscript bibliography (sanity-check before submitting) cat(pr_cite_tree(trees, format = "bibtex"))

what: Step 4 — Hand the posterior to pigauto
title: From species names to a phylogenetic posterior — prepR4pcm + pigauto
text: contract. pigauto::multi_impute_trees(df, trees, m_per_tree) takes df (data NAs) trees (multiPhylo) returns m_per_tree complete-data imputations per tree. output knows imputation came tree, pigauto’s pooling step later uses index. reported intervals wider equivalent single-tree-single-imputation pipeline — correctly . narrower intervals always overconfident.
code: library(pigauto)  # 5 imputations per tree × 50 trees = 250 complete datasets mi <- multi_impute_trees(rec$aligned,                          trees      = trees$tree,                          m_per_tree = 5)  # Fit your model to each completed dataset. Replace the formula # with whatever your hypothesis is. fits <- with_imputations(mi, function(df) {   glmmTMB::glmmTMB(body_size ~ 1 + (1 | species),                    data = df) })  # Pool via Rubin's rules — the standard errors reflect BOTH the # imputation uncertainty AND the tree-posterior uncertainty. pooled <- pool_mi(fits,                   coef_fun = function(fit) fixef(fit)$cond,                   vcov_fun = function(fit) vcov(fit)$cond)  pooled

what: prepR4pcm: reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods
title: prepR4pcm: reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods
text: Species names data rarely match tip labels phylogenetic tree exactly. mismatches prevent combining species traits (data tables) evolutionary relationships (phylogenetic tree) — phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs, e.g. studies trait evolution, niche conservatism, correlated trait change) need. Three kinds mismatch silently drop species analyses: Formatting differences, e.g. Homo_sapiens vs Homo sapiens, trailing whitespace, capitalisation Taxonomic synonyms / different ranks, e.g. Homo sapiens vs Homo sapiens sapiens, recent name vs historical synonym used tree Simple typos, e.g. Homo sapiens vs Hamo sapiens prepR4pcm detects resolves three multi-stage matching cascade (exact → normalised → synonym → fuzzy), documents every decision choices auditable, produces aligned data–tree pairs ready phylogenetic generalised least squares (PGLS), phylogenetic mixed models (PGLMMs), PCM. ’ll find: install, quick example, typical workflow, vignettes covering realistic pipelines, citation information, list bundled example datasets.

title: prepR4pcm: reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("itchyshin/prepR4pcm")

title: prepR4pcm: reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods
text: Four-stage matching cascade: exact match, normalised match (case, whitespace, underscores), synonym resolution via taxadb (Norman et al. 2020), fuzzy matching genus pre-filtering typos. Full provenance: every name-matching decision recorded reconciliation result, can audit name change later. reconcile_summary(), reconcile_plot(), reconcile_report(), reconcile_suggest() help inspect matches find near-misses. Multi-tree support: reconcile_to_trees() matches dataset several trees ; reconcile_diff() compares results. Crosswalks overrides: taxonomic crosswalk published table mapping species names one taxonomy another (e.g. mapping BirdLife species names BirdTree / Jetz phylogeny names). override table user-supplied two-column data frame (name_x, name_y) forces specific name pairs resolve particular way, bypassing cascade. reconcile_crosswalk() converts published crosswalk override table. Tree augmentation: unresolved species one appears data matching tip tree (cascade couldn’t find via formatting, synonymy, fuzzy matching). reconcile_augment() grafts unresolved species onto tree sister congener (species genus). placement assumption rather result, always run sensitivity analyses — fit downstream model without grafted tips report whether conclusions change.

Italics mark R objects / data files (things disk workspace); plain text marks prepR4pcm functions act . first reconciliation pass produces reconciliation object (audit every name match). review fix; ’re happy, reconcile_apply() produces aligned dataset pruned tree matching species lists — precondition phylogenetic comparative method.
code: *Trait data*  +  *Phylogenetic tree*             |         reconcile_tree()             |             V     *reconciliation*  ----> Review: reconcile_summary() / reconcile_plot() / reconcile_report()             |              ----> Fix:    reconcile_override() / reconcile_suggest()             |         reconcile_apply()             |             V     *Aligned data*  +  *Pruned tree*  ---->  PGLS / PGLMM / any PCM

text: example reconciles avonet_subset (919 species rows AVONET, global bird-trait database; Tobias et al. 2022) tree_jetz (657 tips Jetz et al. 2012 bird phylogeny). produces aligned data frame pruned tree ready downstream modelling — sides species, matched order, ready PGLS phylogenetic mixed model. just happened: reconcile_tree() matched every species name avonet_subset$Species1 tip labels tree_jetz, trying exact matches first falling back normalised, synonym, fuzzy matches needed. printed rec object shows count match category. reconcile_apply() takes reconciliation produces () data frame rows restricted species resolved tree tip, (b) tree pruned tips. nrow() ape::Ntip() calls confirm two sides agree species count — precondition downstream PGLS PGLMM call.

Unresolved (x only):262 (28.5%) #> ! Unresolved (y only):0 #> ! Flagged for review: 0 #> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table.  # Apply the reconciliation: aligned data + pruned tree aligned <- reconcile_apply(rec, data = avonet_subset, tree = tree_jetz,                            species_col = "Species1", drop_unresolved = TRUE) #> ! Dropped 262 rows with unresolved species from data #> ℹ Tree has 657 tips after alignment nrow(aligned$data) #> [1] 657 ape::Ntip(aligned$tree) #> [1] 657

title: Retrieve a candidate phylogeny for a species list — pr_get_tree
code: pr_get_tree(   x,   source = c("rotl", "rtrees", "clootl", "fishtree", "datelife", "auto"),   species_col = NULL,   taxon = NULL,   n_tree = 1L,   cache = FALSE,   tnrs = c("auto", "always", "never"),   min_match = 0.8,   check_ultrametric = TRUE,   resolve_polytomies = FALSE,   branch_lengths = NULL,   ... )

title: Retrieve a candidate phylogeny for a species list — pr_get_tree
text: x One : reconciliation object returned reconcile_tree() reconcile_data(); species taken reconciled name_y column NAs unresolved entries dropped. character vector used directly deduplication NA removal. data frame species_col must name character column; unique non-NA values used. source length-1 character vector. external backend use. One : "rotl" Open Tree Life synthesis tree, via CRAN package rotl. Universal taxonomic coverage; calls tnrs_match_names() resolve names OTT ids tol_induced_subtree(). "rtrees" Taxon-specific mega-trees (bird, mammal, fish, amphibian, reptile, plant, shark/ray, bee, butterfly) via GitHub package rtrees (https://daijiang.github.io/rtrees/). Requires taxon = "<group>". Calls get_tree(). Install pak::pak("daijiang/rtrees") (GitHub-). "clootl" Bird-phylogenies current Clements taxonomy, via GitHub package clootl (https://github.com/eliotmiller/clootl). Calls extractTree(). Install pak::pak("eliotmiller/clootl"). "fishtree" Fish-time-calibrated phylogeny (Rabosky et al. 2018), via CRAN package fishtree. Calls fishtree_phylogeny() (single tree) fishtree_complete_phylogeny() (multi-tree posterior; triggered n_tree > 1). Requires exact name matches Fish Tree Life taxonomy — pre-clean reconcile_data() (taxadb authority) best results. "datelife" Universal database pre-computed chronograms (Sanchez Reyes et al. 2024, Syst. Biol. 73:470), via GitHub package datelife (https://github.com/phylotastic/datelife). Returns single SDM-summary chronogram default; n_tree > 1, returns multiPhylo many per-source candidate chronograms. Install use pak::pak("phylotastic/datelife") — package GitHub-(archived CRAN 2024 heavy transitive dep tree pak auto-resolve), prepR4pcm pull via Suggests. "auto" Fall-dispatcher: try installed backends priority order (rtrees taxon provided, rotl, fishtree, clootl, datelife), return first result resolves least min_match species. Useful first-pass exploration yet know backend covers taxa. species_col length-1 character vector. Required x data frame; ignored otherwise. taxon length-1 character vector. Required source = "rtrees". One "bird", "mammal", "fish", "amphibian", "reptile", "plant", "shark_ray", "bee", "butterfly" (see rtrees package help get_tree). Ignored backends. n_tree length-1 positive integer. many trees request backend. Default 1L (single phylo back-compat). backend negotiates differently: "rotl" Always returns 1 (synthesis tree). one-shot warning emitted n_tree > 1. "rtrees" n_tree informational . rtrees::get_tree() n_tree argument; multi-tree count fixed mega-tree selected. Reference trees rtrees uses internally: birds = Jetz et al. 2012 (https://birdtree.org, 100 posterior trees); mammals = Upham et al. 2019 (VertLife, 100 default; set mammal_tree = "phylacine" PHYLACINE set); amphibians + squamates = VertLife; fish = Rabosky et al. 2018 (also wrapped source = "fishtree"); plants = V.PhyloMaker; bees = Bee Tree Life. Override mega-tree used via ... (e.g. bee_tree = "bootstrap" 100 bee trees instead single ML tree). Requires taxon. "clootl" n_tree = 1 calls clootl::extractTree(); n_tree > 1 calls clootl::sampleTrees(count = n_tree) (capped 100 upstream). require AvesData repo set via clootl::get_avesdata_repo() first. "fishtree" Single phylo via fishtree_phylogeny() n_tree = 1; switches fishtree_complete_phylogeny() returning multiPhylo stochastically polytomy-resolved trees n_tree > 1. "datelife" summary_format = "phylo_sdm" (single summary chronogram) n_tree = 1; switches summary_format = "phylo_all" (one chronogram per source, capped n_tree) n_tree > 1. request returns multiPhylo, result's tree slot multiPhylo; otherwise phylo. cache Logical. Cache result disk reuse subsequent identical calls? Default FALSE. TRUE, request keyed (species, source, n_tree, taxon, ...) stored pr_tree_cache_dir(). See pr_tree_cache_status() pr_tree_cache_clear() inspecting / wiping cache. tnrs length-1 character vector. Run TNRS preflight (Open Tree Life name resolution via rotl::tnrs_match_names) species list calling backend? One : "auto" (default) Run TNRS backends — currently clootl fishtree. Improves match rate substantially. "always" Run TNRS regardless backend. "never" Skip TNRS even backend benefit. rotl installed, TNRS silently skipped one-shot warning. min_match length-1 numeric [0, 1]. used source = "auto". minimum fraction input species backend must resolve dispatcher accept result; backend meets threshold, best available returned warning. Default 0.8. check_ultrametric Logical. producing tree, check ultrametric (tips equidistant root) warn . Default TRUE. enforced backends normally return chronograms (rtrees, clootl, fishtree, datelife); rotl returns topology without real branch lengths, check skipped. force ultrametricity non-ultrametric result, use phytools::force.ultrametric() ape::chronos() directly — prepR4pcm modify tree silently. resolve_polytomies Logical. retrieval, resolve polytomies via ape::multi2di() random = TRUE? Default FALSE (back-compat; topology preserved). Useful phylogenetic meta-analysis, strictly bifurcating tree required pr_phylo_cor() / ape::vcv() produce full-rank correlation matrix. branch_lengths length-1 character vector NULL. retrieval (polytomy resolution requested), assign branch lengths via named method? Default NULL (transformation; backend's branch lengths kept -). values: "grafen" Grafen's (1989) method via ape::compute.brlen() method = "Grafen". canonical choice phylogenetic meta-analysis topology comes rotl (whose edge lengths unit-length placeholders). See Cinar et al. (2022) Methods Ecol. Evol. 13:383, use exact pattern. "compute.brlen" "grafen" — Grafen ape::compute.brlen()'s default method. Provided alias users think terms underlying function name. "unit" Set every edge length 1. crudest option; useful sensitivity-analysis comparisons. ... Backend-specific arguments forwarded underlying call. See help page underlying function relevant backend package (tol_induced_subtree rotl, extractTree clootl, get_tree rtrees, fishtree_phylogeny / fishtree_complete_phylogeny fishtree, datelife_search datelife) full list.

text: Apply sequence deterministic text transformations scientific names differ formatting compare equal. routine used stage 2 matching cascade reconcile_data() reconcile_tree(). Polytomies resolved random (resolve_polytomies = TRUE). Branch lengths computed via Grafen's method (branch_lengths = "grafen") rotl's edge lengths unit-length placeholders. correlation matrix computed reused random = ~1|species's R = list(species = phy_cor) metafor::rma.mv() (random = ~species cov.formula = ~ phylo MCMCglmm). correlation matrix property , Brownian-motion model tree branch lengths time units, two species' -diagonal entry equals time root MRCA divided time root tip. ultrametric tree always diagonal = 1 (every tip distance root). meta-analysis rotl topology + Grafen's method, resulting matrix standard Pagel's lambda = 1 phylogenetic correlation metafor::rma.mv() accepts directly.

code: # Two trees with identical tip sets set.seed(1) t1 <- ape::rtree(10) t2 <- ape::rtree(10, tip.label = t1$tip.label) cmp <- pr_tree_compare(t1, t2) cmp$n_shared #> [1] 10 cmp$pairwise_rf #>       tree1 tree2 #> tree1     0    16 #> tree2    16     0  # Two trees with overlapping but not identical tips t3 <- ape::rtree(8, tip.label = t1$tip.label[1:8]) cmp <- pr_tree_compare(t1, t3) cmp$pairwise_jaccard #>       tree1 tree2 #> tree1   1.0   0.8 #> tree2   0.8   1.0

title: Valid taxonomic authorities — pr_valid_authorities
text: "col" Catalogue Life. default sensible starting point taxa. "itis" Integrated Taxonomic Information System. Strong coverage North American vertebrates plants. "gbif" GBIF backbone. Wider coverage; captures recent synonymy. "ncbi" NCBI Taxonomy. Best working sequence data. "ott" Open Tree Life synthetic taxonomy. Useful downstream phylogeny Open Tree synthesis. restrict schema "dwc" (Darwin Core) calling taxadb::td_create() "common" schema ship OTT taxadb v22.12. "itis_test" small bundled subset ITIS, cached locally taxadb testing. Intended examples unit tests; analysis. Five authority codes previous versions package advertised — iucn, tpl, fb, slb, wd — list. Empirical testing taxadb v22.12 showed iucn errors schema mismatch four taxadb providers . Anyone passing one values getting hard error; passing now produces helpful migration message instead.

title: prepR4pcm: Reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods — prepR4pcm-package
text: Species names dataset rarely match tip labels phylogenetic tree. Formatting differences (Homo_sapiens vs Homo sapiens), taxonomic synonymy (Corvus brachyrhynchos splits lumps), simple spelling mistakes silently drop species PGLS, phylogenetic mixed models, phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs). prepR4pcm toolkit ecologists evolutionary biologists detect resolve mismatches, audit every decision, produce aligned data-tree pairs ready downstream analysis.

title: prepR4pcm: Reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods — prepR4pcm-package
text: minimal end--end pipeline looks like :
code: # 1. Match your data frame to a tree rec <- reconcile_tree(   avonet_subset, tree_jetz,   x_species = "Species1",   fuzzy     = TRUE          # enable typo correction )  # 2. Review what matched, what is flagged, what is unresolved reconcile_summary(rec) reconcile_plot(rec) reconcile_suggest(rec)      # suggest near-misses for unresolved names  # 3. Correct any unresolved or flagged cases by hand rec <- reconcile_override(rec,         name_x = "Corvus brachyrhnchos",  # typo in data         name_y = "Corvus_brachyrhynchos")  # 4. Produce an aligned dataset and pruned tree aligned <- reconcile_apply(rec,                            data = avonet_subset, tree = tree_jetz,                            species_col = "Species1",                            drop_unresolved = TRUE)  # 5. Ready for PCM # caper::pgls(Mass ~ Wing.Length, data = aligned$data, tree = aligned$tree)

title: prepR4pcm: Reconcile species names for phylogenetic comparative methods — prepR4pcm-package
text: Reconciliation object central data structure. Contains mapping tibble (one row per source name, match type score), meta list (reproducibility provenance), counts summary, overrides log applied manual corrections, unused_overrides audit trail overrides applied (e.g. name_y missing target). Returned reconcile_* matching functions. Inspect reconcile_summary(), extract table reconcile_mapping(), act reconcile_apply(), reconcile_merge(), reconcile_export(). Four-stage matching cascade Names resolved order, first stage produces match recorded match_type: exact — verbatim string equality. normalized — removing underscores, fixing case, stripping authority strings (Corvus corax Linnaeus 1758), applying diacritic folding. synonym — via local taxonomic database (see taxadb) Catalogue Life GBIF. fuzzy — character-level similarity remaining unmatched names (opt-via fuzzy = TRUE). additional overrides manual edits applied top match_type = "manual". Provenance Every decision logged mapping table (match_type, match_score, match_source) meta (package version, timestamp, taxonomic authority, fuzzy threshold, etc.). Use reconcile_report() produce shareable HTML audit trail supplementary materials collaborators. Splits lumps Taxonomic revisions often split one species several, lump several one. reconcile_splits_lumps() flags cases can decide handle analysis. Tree augmentation unresolved species congeners tree, reconcile_augment() can graft sister taxa genus level. exploratory aid: always run sensitivity analyses without augmented tips.

text: datasets named list data frames. names used dataset labels (e.g. morpho, nests, plumage) output. tree ape::phylo object, path Newick/Nexus file. species_cols Character vector. Species column name dataset. length 1, column name used every dataset. Auto-detected data frame NULL. authority length-1 character vector, NULL. Taxonomic authority used synonym resolution (stage 3 cascade). One : "col" (default) Catalogue Life — broad, curated, frequently updated. sensible default taxa. "itis" Integrated Taxonomic Information System — strong North American vertebrates plants. "gbif" Global Biodiversity Information Facility backbone. Wider coverage; includes recent synonymy. "ncbi" NCBI Taxonomy — best working sequence data. "ott" Open Tree Life synthetic taxonomy. Useful downstream phylogeny Open Tree synthesis. "itis_test" small bundled subset ITIS, cached locally taxadb testing. Intended examples unit tests; analysis. NULL Skip synonym stage entirely. Useful quick checks taxadb unavailable. Stages 1, 2 4 still run. Five authority codes earlier versions package advertised — "iucn", "tpl", "fb", "slb", "wd" — longer accepted. Empirical testing taxadb v22.12 showed iucn errors schema mismatch others taxadb providers . Passing one values now produces helpful migration error. rank length-1 character vector. Controls trinomials handled normalisation: "species" (default) Strip infraspecific epithets "Parus major major" becomes "Parus major" matching. "subspecies" Keep trinomials intact. Use analysis operates subspecies level. overrides Optional pre-built corrections. Either data frame least columns name_x name_y (plus optional user_note column), file path CSV columns. name listed bypasses cascade recorded match_type = "manual". Useful applying published crosswalks (see reconcile_crosswalk()) locking decisions made previous run. db_version length-1 character vector. taxadb database snapshot use (e.g. "22.12"). NULL (default) uses latest available. fuzzy Logical. Enables fuzzy-matching stage TRUE. Default FALSE. Turn catch likely typos (Corvus brachyrhnchos -> Corvus brachyrhynchos). FALSE, stages 1–3 still run. fuzzy_threshold Numeric [0, 1]. Minimum genus-weighted similarity score fuzzy match accepted. Default 0.9 (roughly "~10% characters differ"). Lower values (e.g. 0.7) permissive produce false positives; always review fuzzy matches reconcile_suggest() reconcile_review() trusting . resolve length-1 character vector. borderline matches: "flag" (default) Mark low-confidence fuzzy matches (score flag_threshold) names indirect taxadb synonymy match_type = "flagged" can audit reconcile_review() reconcile_suggest(). text: reconciliation object meta$type == "data_tree". mapping tibble one row per unique name: matched species (in_x & in_y), data-orphans (in_x & !in_y, candidates reconcile_augment()), tree-orphans (!in_x & in_y, candidates reconcile_apply() prune).

title: Reconcile species names between a dataset and a phylogenetic tree — reconcile_tree
text: Internally, reconcile_tree() treats tree's tip labels y argument reconcile_data() runs four-stage matching cascade (exact -> normalized -> synonym -> fuzzy). Tip labels typically differ data names formatting (underscores, capitalisation, authority strings), even authority = NULL usually recover matches normalized stage. Turn fuzzy = TRUE also catch spelling mistakes. reconciliation, typical workflow : Inspect reconcile_summary() reconcile_plot(). Investigate unresolved names reconcile_suggest() fix reconcile_override() reconcile_override_batch(). Produce aligned data frame pruned tree via reconcile_apply(). Optionally, graft orphan species onto tree reconcile_augment() (exploratory ; always run sensitivity analyses).

title: Reconcile tip labels between two phylogenetic trees — reconcile_trees
text: tree1 ape::phylo object, character(1) path Newick/Nexus tree file. tree2 ape::phylo object, character(1) path Newick/Nexus tree file. authority length-1 character vector, NULL. Taxonomic authority used synonym resolution (stage 3 cascade). One : "col" (default) Catalogue Life — broad, curated, frequently updated. sensible default taxa. "itis" Integrated Taxonomic Information System — strong North American vertebrates plants. "gbif" Global Biodiversity Information Facility backbone. Wider coverage; includes recent synonymy. "ncbi" NCBI Taxonomy — best working sequence data. "ott" Open Tree Life synthetic taxonomy. Useful downstream phylogeny Open Tree synthesis. "itis_test" small bundled subset ITIS, cached locally taxadb testing. Intended examples unit tests; analysis. NULL Skip synonym stage entirely. Useful quick checks taxadb unavailable. Stages 1, 2 4 still run. Five authority codes earlier versions package advertised — "iucn", "tpl", "fb", "slb", "wd" — longer accepted. Empirical testing taxadb v22.12 showed iucn errors schema mismatch others taxadb providers . Passing one values now produces helpful migration error. rank length-1 character vector. Controls trinomials handled normalisation: "species" (default) Strip infraspecific epithets "Parus major major" becomes "Parus major" matching. "subspecies" Keep trinomials intact. Use analysis operates subspecies level. overrides Optional pre-built corrections. Either data frame least columns name_x name_y (plus optional user_note column), file path CSV columns. name listed bypasses cascade recorded match_type = "manual". Useful applying published crosswalks (see reconcile_crosswalk()) locking decisions made previous run. db_version length-1 character vector. taxadb database snapshot use (e.g. "22.12"). NULL (default) uses latest available. fuzzy Logical. Enables fuzzy-matching stage TRUE. Default FALSE. Turn catch likely typos (Corvus brachyrhnchos -> Corvus brachyrhynchos). FALSE, stages 1–3 still run. fuzzy_threshold Numeric [0, 1]. Minimum genus-weighted similarity score fuzzy match accepted. Default 0.9 (roughly "~10% characters differ"). Lower values (e.g. 0.7) permissive produce false positives; always review fuzzy matches reconcile_suggest() reconcile_review() trusting . resolve length-1 character vector. borderline matches: "flag" (default) Mark low-confidence fuzzy matches (score flag_threshold) names indirect taxadb synonymy match_type = "flagged" can audit reconcile_review() reconcile_suggest(). "first" Accept highest-scoring candidate silently, without flagging. Faster riskier; use already reviewed ambiguities. quiet Logical. Suppresses progress messages TRUE. Default FALSE.
